_schema: Consumer/contractor financing providers — Phase 2 category added 2026-05-28. Point-of-sale financing + lender marketplaces that let contractors offer monthly payments on big-ticket jobs. Grouped by financing_model. Rates/fees verified-as-of verified_date and change often; re-verify quarterly. affiliate_url stays 'TBD' until tracking is confirmed.

providers.name: Wisetack
providers.tagline: Embedded consumer financing built for home-service businesses
providers.pricing_summary: Contractor pays a 3.9% merchant fee per financed transaction (higher only when the customer selects an extended 0% APR add-on). Customer APRs range 0%–35.9% based on amount and credit
providers.contractor_cost: 3.9% merchant fee per transaction; higher only on extended 0% APR add-ons
providers.long_description:
Wisetack is the consumer-financing platform built specifically for home-service businesses, and it's the one most contractors actually touch because it's embedded directly inside the major FSM tools — Housecall Pro, Jobber, FieldPulse, Arborgold, Contractor+ and others all offer Wisetack natively. Founded in 2018 in San Francisco, it turns 'that's a $9,000 repair' into 'that's about $180/month,' which measurably lifts close rates on big-ticket work.

The flow is dead simple: the contractor sends a text or invoice link, the customer applies from their phone, gets a near-instant decision 24/7, and checking options doesn't impact their credit. Offers range 0%–35.9% APR based on amount and creditworthiness, and the standard merchant approval includes a 3-month 0% APR option, with 6, 12, and 24-month 0% APR terms available as add-ons.

The cost model is refreshingly clear: a flat 3.9% merchant fee per financed transaction, with no hidden fees — and you only pay the higher fee when a customer takes an extended 0% APR add-on. The trade-offs: loan amounts top out lower than home-equity-scale lenders (it's built for service tickets, not $80K remodels), and 3.9% is a real cost to factor into pricing. But for an HVAC, plumbing, electrical, or roofing shop that wants frictionless point-of-sale financing wired into the FSM it already uses, Wisetack is the default.
providers.how_it_works: From inside your FSM (or the Wisetack merchant portal), you send the customer a financing link by text or on the invoice. The customer applies on their phone in a couple of minutes, gets a near-instant decision 24/7, and sees their monthly-payment options — and the soft credit check doesn't affect their score. They pick a plan, you book the job, and Wisetack pays you while the customer pays Wisetack over time. You pay a flat 3.9% merchant fee per financed transaction; the standard approval includes a 3-month 0% APR option, and you can enable 6/12/24-month 0% APR add-ons (which carry a higher merchant fee) to close larger jobs.

pros_detail.title: Embedded in the FSM you already use
pros_detail.explanation: Wisetack is built into Housecall Pro, Jobber, FieldPulse, and more, so offering financing is a native part of your existing invoice/checkout flow — no separate system to learn or bolt on.

pros_detail.title: Frictionless, credit-safe application
pros_detail.explanation: Customers apply by text in minutes, get a near-instant 24/7 decision, and checking options doesn't impact their credit. That low friction is exactly what closes a hesitant customer at the kitchen table.

pros_detail.title: Transparent, flat 3.9% fee
pros_detail.explanation: A single flat merchant fee with no hidden charges is unusually clear for financing, and you only pay the higher rate when a customer takes an extended 0% APR add-on — so costs are predictable.

pros_detail.title: Lifts close rates on big-ticket jobs
pros_detail.explanation: Reframing a large repair as a monthly payment measurably increases conversion on high-ticket HVAC/roofing/plumbing work — often paying for the 3.9% fee many times over in closed jobs.

pros_detail.title: Built for home services specifically
pros_detail.explanation: Unlike general lenders, Wisetack is designed around service-business tickets and workflows, so the amounts, terms, and UX fit how trades actually sell and invoice.

cons_detail.title: Lower loan ceiling than home-equity lenders
cons_detail.explanation: Wisetack is built for service tickets (commonly up to ~$25,000), not large remodels. For $50K–$100K projects, a home-improvement lender or marketplace (GreenSky, Hearth, Acorn) with higher limits fits better.

cons_detail.title: 3.9% is a real cost
cons_detail.explanation: The merchant fee comes out of your margin, so you need to price financing into jobs or accept the cost as a conversion expense. On thin-margin work it adds up.

cons_detail.title: Extended 0% APR costs more
cons_detail.explanation: The attractive 6/12/24-month 0% APR add-ons carry a higher merchant fee than the standard plan, so offering long interest-free terms increases your cost per financed job.

cons_detail.title: Customer APR can be high
cons_detail.explanation: Beyond promotional 0% terms, customer APRs reach up to 35.9% based on credit — so for some customers the financing is expensive, which can affect satisfaction if not framed clearly.

cons_detail.title: Approval depends on customer credit
cons_detail.explanation: Not every customer qualifies or gets the best terms; approvals and rates hinge on creditworthiness, so financing won't close every big-ticket job on its own.

providers.best_for: HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and roofing contractors who want frictionless point-of-sale financing wired into the FSM they already use, to close more big-ticket service jobs
providers.weaknesses: Loan amounts top out lower than home-equity-scale lenders (built for service tickets, not $80K remodels); the 3.9% merchant fee is a real margin cost; extended 0% APR add-ons cost more

ratings.reddit_sentiment: well-regarded among home-service contractors; valued for the embedded FSM experience and transparent flat fee

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Wisetack

faqs.q: How much does Wisetack cost the contractor?
faqs.a: Wisetack charges a flat 3.9% merchant fee per financed transaction, with no hidden fees. You only pay a higher fee when a customer selects an extended 0% APR add-on (6, 12, or 24 months). The standard approval includes a 3-month 0% APR option at the base rate.

faqs.q: Does applying for Wisetack hurt my customer's credit?
faqs.a: No — checking financing options with Wisetack uses a soft credit check that does not impact the customer's credit score. They apply by text in a couple of minutes and get a near-instant decision 24/7, which is a big part of why it closes hesitant customers.

faqs.q: How is Wisetack different from GreenSky or Hearth?
faqs.a: Wisetack is embedded point-of-sale financing for service tickets, built into FSM tools, with a flat 3.9% merchant fee and amounts up to ~$25,000. GreenSky is home-improvement financing up to $100,000 with dealer fees of 3–15%. Hearth is a lender marketplace that charges contractors a yearly subscription. For service-ticket financing inside your FSM, Wisetack is usually the simplest.

faqs.q: What can my customers finance with Wisetack?
faqs.a: Home-service jobs — HVAC replacements and repairs, plumbing, electrical, roofing, and similar work — typically up to around $25,000. It's designed for service tickets rather than large whole-home remodels, where a higher-limit home-improvement lender fits better.

faqs.q: Will Wisetack work with my software?
faqs.a: Likely yes — Wisetack is natively integrated into many field-service platforms including Housecall Pro, Jobber, and FieldPulse, and is also available via its own merchant portal. If you're on one of those FSMs, you can usually turn on Wisetack financing without adding a separate system.

providers.name: GreenSky
providers.pricing_summary: Bank-funded home-improvement loans up to $100,000. Contractor pays a dealer fee of 3–15% (deducted from the loan): roughly 3–6% on standard loans, 8–15% on 0% promotional plans. Customers get fixed-rate, deferred-interest, or 0% options
providers.apr_range: Fixed-rate, deferred-interest, and 0% promotional plans (varies by program)
providers.contractor_cost: Dealer fee 3–15%, deducted from the loan amount (3–6% standard; 8–15% on 0% promos)
providers.long_description:
GreenSky is one of the largest home-improvement point-of-sale financing programs in the US, funding loans up to $100,000 through partner banks. Founded in 2006 in Atlanta (owned by Goldman Sachs, then sold to a Sixth Street-led group in 2024), it's the established incumbent that big remodeling, roofing, HVAC, and solar contractors have used for years to offer financing on large projects — well beyond the service-ticket range of a tool like Wisetack.

GreenSky doesn't lend directly; the contractor presents GreenSky plans with a quote, the customer applies, and once approved the loan pays the contractor while the customer repays the bank. It offers a wide range of plans — fixed-rate loans, deferred-interest loans, and 0% promotional financing — which is why it fits large, varied home-improvement work.

The honest concerns are cost and consumer-friendliness. Contractors pay dealer fees of roughly 3–15% (deducted from the loan), with 0% promotional plans carrying the highest fees (8–15%) — a major margin consideration. And the deferred-interest products can hit a customer with retroactive interest if they don't pay the balance off within the promo window, which has drawn consumer criticism. GreenSky is a powerful tool for financing big projects at scale, but contractors should weigh the high dealer fees, and be transparent with customers about deferred-interest terms rather than just selling the headline '0%.'
providers.how_it_works: GreenSky is bank-funded, not a direct lender. You enroll as a merchant/dealer, then present GreenSky financing plans alongside your quote on a big project. The customer applies (name, address, SSN, income), and once approved you show their plan options — fixed-rate, deferred-interest, or 0% promotional. When the customer approves the transaction, the loan pays you (minus the dealer fee, which is deducted from the loan amount), and the customer repays GreenSky's partner bank over time. Dealer fees run 3–6% on standard loans and 8–15% on 0% promotions, so the plan type you offer directly affects your cost.

pros_detail.title: High loan amounts (up to $100K)
pros_detail.explanation: GreenSky funds large home-improvement projects up to $100,000, far beyond service-ticket tools. For big remodels, roofing, or solar, it can finance the whole job in one loan.

pros_detail.title: Established, large-scale program
pros_detail.explanation: Operating since 2006 with bank backing, GreenSky is a mature, widely-used program with broad lender capacity — contractors doing high volume of large projects rely on its scale.

pros_detail.title: Wide range of plan types
pros_detail.explanation: Fixed-rate, deferred-interest, and 0% promotional plans give flexibility to match the offer to the customer and project, which helps close varied large jobs.

pros_detail.title: Contractor paid by the loan
pros_detail.explanation: Once the customer approves, the loan funds the contractor — you're not waiting on the customer's installment payments, so cash flow on big projects stays healthy.

pros_detail.title: Recognized by customers
pros_detail.explanation: As a large, established brand, GreenSky is a name many homeowners recognize, which can add a degree of trust to the financing offer on a big-ticket project.

cons_detail.title: High dealer fees
cons_detail.explanation: Contractors pay 3–15% dealer fees deducted from the loan, with 0% promotional plans at the high end (8–15%). On a large project, that's a substantial cost that must be priced in — far more than Wisetack's flat 3.9% on smaller tickets.

cons_detail.title: Deferred-interest risk to customers
cons_detail.explanation: Deferred-interest plans can retroactively charge the customer all the accrued interest if they don't pay off the balance within the promo window. That's drawn consumer criticism, and contractors should disclose it clearly rather than just selling '0%.'

cons_detail.title: More complex enrollment
cons_detail.explanation: GreenSky is a dealer/merchant program with more onboarding than a self-serve marketplace, and the plan/fee structure takes effort to understand and present correctly.

cons_detail.title: Ownership changes
cons_detail.explanation: GreenSky has changed hands (Goldman Sachs, then a Sixth Street-led group in 2024), and program terms and dealer fees can shift — confirm current terms rather than relying on older information.

cons_detail.title: Best for large projects only
cons_detail.explanation: The dealer-fee economics and $100K capacity suit big-ticket remodels, roofing, and solar; for routine service tickets, a lower-cost tool like Wisetack or a free marketplace usually fits better.

providers.best_for: Remodeling, roofing, HVAC, and solar contractors financing large projects (up to $100,000) who need high loan capacity and can price in higher dealer fees
providers.weaknesses: High dealer fees (3–15%, up to 8–15% on 0% promos); deferred-interest plans can hit customers with retroactive interest; more complex enrollment; terms can shift with ownership changes
providers.reputation_flag: GreenSky's deferred-interest promotions can charge customers retroactive interest if the balance isn't paid off within the promo window, and contractor dealer fees run high (3–15%, with 0% promos at 8–15%). Sell the real terms, not just the headline '0%,' and price the dealer fee into the job.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: established and widely used for big projects, but criticized for high dealer fees and deferred-interest risk to consumers

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from GreenSky

faqs.q: How much does GreenSky cost contractors?
faqs.a: Contractors pay a dealer fee of roughly 3–15%, deducted from the loan amount — about 3–6% on standard loans and 8–15% on 0% promotional plans. So the more attractive the customer offer (like 0% for a long term), the higher your cost. Price the dealer fee into the job.

faqs.q: What is the deferred-interest risk with GreenSky?
faqs.a: Some GreenSky plans use deferred interest: if the customer doesn't pay off the balance within the promotional window, they can be charged all the interest that accrued from day one, retroactively. It's drawn consumer criticism, so contractors should clearly explain these terms rather than just advertising '0%.'

faqs.q: How much can a customer borrow with GreenSky?
faqs.a: Up to $100,000, which is why GreenSky suits large home-improvement projects — full remodels, roofing, solar — that exceed the limits of service-ticket financing like Wisetack (typically up to ~$25,000).

faqs.q: GreenSky vs Wisetack?
faqs.a: GreenSky finances large projects (up to $100K) with dealer fees of 3–15% and a range of plan types including deferred interest; Wisetack is simpler embedded financing for service tickets (up to ~$25K) with a flat 3.9% merchant fee. Big-project contractors lean GreenSky for capacity; service-ticket shops usually prefer Wisetack's simplicity and lower, transparent cost.

faqs.q: Does GreenSky lend the money directly?
faqs.a: No — GreenSky is a financing program funded by partner banks, not a direct lender. The contractor presents GreenSky plans, the customer applies and is approved by the bank, the loan pays the contractor (minus the dealer fee), and the customer repays the bank over time.

providers.name: Hearth
providers.tagline: Contractor financing marketplace plus sales tools (subscription-based)
providers.pricing_summary: Contractor pays a yearly subscription — Essentials $1,499/yr, Pro $1,799/yr, Elite $4,999/yr, plus a one-time $99 setup. Hearth is a licensed broker (not a lender): 18+ lender partners offer customers $1,000–$100,000 at 4.9%–35.99% APR over 2–12 years, plus 0% intro credit cards
providers.apr_range: 4.9%–35.99% APR via 18+ lenders; plus 0% intro credit cards (6–18 mo)
providers.contractor_cost: Subscription: $1,499–$4,999/yr + $99 setup (no per-loan dealer fee; you pay for the platform)
providers.long_description:
Hearth (owned by Shogun Enterprises) is a contractor-focused financing marketplace bundled with sales and marketing tools. Rather than charging a per-loan dealer fee, Hearth charges the contractor a yearly subscription and acts as a licensed broker: it doesn't make loans or credit decisions itself, but routes customers to 18+ lender partners who offer $1,000–$100,000 at 4.9%–35.99% APR over 2–12 years, plus 0% intro credit cards (6–18 months). For the contractor, the pitch is a digital suite — financing, estimates/invoicing, and sales tools — to win more jobs and look professional.

The subscription model is the defining (and divisive) feature. Plans run Essentials $1,499/year, Pro $1,799/year, and Elite $4,999/year, plus a one-time $99 setup. That's a real fixed cost — unlike Wisetack's per-transaction fee or Acorn's free marketplace — so Hearth pays off for contractors who finance enough volume to amortize the subscription and who value the bundled sales tools.

The trade-offs show in the reviews, which are mixed. Contractors praise the tool suite and report more closed jobs, but a recurring complaint is customer service and follow-up — some report losing prequalified projects because Hearth didn't respond. For a contractor who wants a financing marketplace plus sales tools and will use them enough to justify a four-figure annual fee, Hearth can work; volume-light contractors are usually better served by a free marketplace (Acorn) or per-transaction financing (Wisetack).
providers.how_it_works: You subscribe to a Hearth plan ($1,499–$4,999/year + $99 setup) and get a contractor dashboard with financing plus estimating/invoicing and sales tools. Because Hearth is a licensed broker (not a lender), you send customers a financing link; they prequalify with a soft credit check and see offers from Hearth's 18+ lender partners — personal loans of $1,000–$100,000 at 4.9–35.99% APR over 2–12 years, or 0% intro credit cards. The customer picks an offer and is funded by the lender, and you get paid for the job. There's no per-loan dealer fee deducted — you've paid for access via the subscription instead.

pros_detail.title: No per-loan dealer fee
pros_detail.explanation: Unlike GreenSky's 3–15% dealer fees, Hearth doesn't skim a percentage off each loan — you pay a flat subscription instead. For high financing volume, that can be cheaper per job than percentage-based dealer fees.

pros_detail.title: Marketplace of 18+ lenders
pros_detail.explanation: Customers see offers from many lenders ($1,000–$100,000, 4.9–35.99% APR), increasing the odds of an approval and a workable payment for a range of credit profiles and project sizes.

pros_detail.title: Bundled sales tools
pros_detail.explanation: Hearth pairs financing with estimating/invoicing and sales tools, so contractors who want a financing-plus-sales suite get more than a standalone financing link.

pros_detail.title: Soft-pull prequalification
pros_detail.explanation: Customers prequalify with a soft credit check that doesn't hurt their score, lowering friction to explore monthly-payment options on a quote.

pros_detail.title: Wide loan range
pros_detail.explanation: From $1,000 service work to $100,000 remodels, plus 0% intro credit cards, Hearth's lender network spans small and large jobs in one platform.

cons_detail.title: Four-figure annual subscription
cons_detail.explanation: At $1,499–$4,999/year plus $99 setup, Hearth is a fixed cost regardless of how much you finance. Contractors who don't run enough financing volume to amortize it lose money versus free or per-transaction options.

cons_detail.title: Customer-service / follow-up complaints
cons_detail.explanation: A recurring negative theme is poor support and follow-up — some contractors report losing prequalified projects because Hearth didn't respond. For a tool you're paying four figures for, that's a real concern to vet.

cons_detail.title: Broker, not lender
cons_detail.explanation: Hearth doesn't make loans or set rates — lender partners do. That's normal for a marketplace, but it means approval, terms, and the customer experience depend on third-party lenders, not Hearth.

cons_detail.title: Cost vs free marketplaces
cons_detail.explanation: Acorn Finance offers a similar lender-marketplace model with no contractor fee at all. Hearth's subscription needs to be justified by its bundled sales tools and your volume, or a free marketplace is the better deal.

cons_detail.title: Mixed overall reputation
cons_detail.explanation: Reviews are genuinely mixed — strong for the tools and results for some, weak on service for others. Trial it and check recent reviews before committing to an annual plan.

providers.best_for: Contractors who finance enough volume to amortize a four-figure annual subscription and want a lender marketplace bundled with estimating and sales tools
providers.weaknesses: Four-figure annual subscription is a fixed cost regardless of volume; recurring customer-service and follow-up complaints; a free marketplace (Acorn) offers a similar model at no contractor cost

ratings.reddit_sentiment: mixed — praised for the tool suite and added jobs, but recurring complaints about customer service and lead follow-up

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Hearth

faqs.q: How does Hearth charge contractors?
faqs.a: Hearth uses a yearly subscription rather than a per-loan dealer fee: Essentials $1,499/yr, Pro $1,799/yr, and Elite $4,999/yr, plus a one-time $99 setup. You pay for platform access and bundled sales tools; there's no percentage skimmed off each loan like GreenSky's dealer fees.

faqs.q: Does Hearth make the loans?
faqs.a: No — Hearth is a licensed broker, not a lender. It doesn't accept credit applications, make loans, or set rates; its 18+ lender partners provide the APRs (4.9–35.99%), terms (2–12 years), and fees. Hearth routes customers to those lenders and bundles sales tools around the experience.

faqs.q: Is Hearth worth the subscription?
faqs.a: It depends on your financing volume and whether you'll use the bundled estimating/sales tools. Because the $1,499–$4,999/year fee is fixed, contractors who finance a lot of jobs can come out ahead of percentage dealer fees, while low-volume contractors usually do better with a free marketplace like Acorn Finance or per-transaction financing like Wisetack.

faqs.q: What are the main complaints about Hearth?
faqs.a: The most common negative theme is customer service and follow-up — some contractors report losing prequalified projects because Hearth didn't respond or follow up with clients. Given the four-figure annual cost, it's worth checking recent reviews and trialing the support experience before committing.

faqs.q: Hearth vs Acorn Finance?
faqs.a: Both are lender marketplaces with soft-pull prequalification, but the cost model differs sharply: Hearth charges contractors a $1,499+/year subscription (bundled with sales tools), while Acorn Finance is free to contractors with no subscription or dealer fees. If you want the bundled tools and have the volume, Hearth can fit; if you just want free lender-marketplace financing, Acorn is the cheaper path.

providers.name: Acorn Finance
providers.tagline: Free lender marketplace — compare 30+ home-improvement loan offers
providers.pricing_summary: Free for contractors — no dealer, subscription, sign-up, or credit-card fees. Customers compare prequalified fixed-rate offers from 30+ lenders via a soft credit check; the contractor is paid upfront and lenders fund within 48 hours of approval
providers.apr_range: Fixed-rate offers from 30+ lenders (rate depends on customer credit)
providers.loan_range: Home-improvement loan amounts vary by lender
providers.contractor_cost: Free — no dealer, subscription, sign-up, or credit-card fees
providers.long_description:
Acorn Finance is an embedded home-improvement lending marketplace that's genuinely free for contractors — no dealer fees, no subscription, no sign-up or credit-card fees. Founded in 2017 in Sacramento (formerly Headway Sales), it lets a contractor create an account in minutes and get a personalized financing link to share with customers, who then see prequalified, fixed-rate offers from 30+ top consumer lenders in about 60 seconds with no impact to their credit.

The model is contractor-friendly in two important ways: it's free, and the contractor is paid upfront once a customer accepts an offer and is funded — no waiting on installments. Acorn's lender partners are required to deliver funds within 48 hours of approval, which can speed up job starts. Because customers compare offers from many lenders, they often find a better fixed rate than a single deferred-interest or promotional program, and the soft-pull comparison shopping is transparent.

The trade-offs: as a marketplace of personal-loan lenders, Acorn's offers are fixed-rate personal loans rather than the embedded 0%-promo and deferred-interest products of GreenSky, and it's less deeply embedded in FSM checkouts than Wisetack. But for a contractor who simply wants to offer financing at zero cost, let customers shop real fixed-rate offers, and get paid upfront, Acorn Finance is one of the most contractor-aligned options in the category.
providers.how_it_works: You create a free Acorn account in minutes and get a personalized financing link (no fees or obligations) to share with customers. The customer enters their info and, via a soft credit check (no score impact), sees prequalified fixed-rate offers from Acorn's 30+ lender partners in about 60 seconds. They pick the offer that fits their budget, get funded (lenders are required to fund within 48 hours of approval), and you — the contractor — are paid upfront for the job rather than waiting on the customer's installments. There's no dealer fee, subscription, or sign-up cost to you; Acorn's revenue comes from the lender side of the marketplace.

pros_detail.title: Free for contractors
pros_detail.explanation: No dealer fee, no subscription, no sign-up or credit-card fees. Unlike GreenSky (3–15% dealer fees) or Hearth ($1,499+/yr subscription), offering Acorn financing costs the contractor nothing — pure upside on close rate.

pros_detail.title: Customers compare 30+ lenders
pros_detail.explanation: Prequalified offers from many lenders in ~60 seconds mean higher approval odds and often a better fixed rate for the customer than a single program — transparent comparison shopping with no credit impact.

pros_detail.title: Contractor paid upfront
pros_detail.explanation: Once the customer is funded, you're paid upfront rather than waiting on installments, which protects cash flow on larger jobs.

pros_detail.title: Fast funding (48 hours)
pros_detail.explanation: Lender partners must deliver funds within 48 hours of approval, which can move up job start dates and reduce the limbo between sale and start.

pros_detail.title: Quick, no-obligation setup
pros_detail.explanation: Contractors can create an account and get a sharable financing link in minutes with no fees or commitment, so there's almost no barrier to start offering financing.

cons_detail.title: Fixed-rate personal loans, not embedded 0% promos
cons_detail.explanation: Acorn's offers are fixed-rate personal loans from lenders, not the embedded 0%-promotional and deferred-interest products GreenSky offers. Contractors who lean on '0% for 12 months' promos as a sales hook won't find the same structure here.

cons_detail.title: Less embedded than Wisetack
cons_detail.explanation: Acorn works via a shareable link rather than being deeply built into FSM checkouts like Wisetack. It integrates with some tools (e.g., JobTread), but the in-software experience is lighter.

cons_detail.title: Rates depend on customer credit
cons_detail.explanation: As with all financing, the customer's offers and APRs depend on their credit; not every customer gets a low rate, so financing won't close every job.

cons_detail.title: No bundled sales tools
cons_detail.explanation: Acorn is a focused financing marketplace, not a suite — it doesn't bundle estimating/sales tools like Hearth. That's fine if you only want financing, but it's not an all-in-one.

cons_detail.title: Marketplace, so experience varies by lender
cons_detail.explanation: Because funding and servicing happen through third-party lenders, the customer's post-approval experience depends on which lender they choose, not on Acorn directly.

providers.best_for: Contractors who want to offer financing at zero cost, let customers compare real fixed-rate offers from many lenders, and get paid upfront — without a dealer fee or subscription
providers.weaknesses: Offers are fixed-rate personal loans rather than embedded 0%-promo/deferred-interest products; less deeply embedded in FSM checkouts than Wisetack; no bundled sales tools

ratings.reddit_sentiment: viewed positively for being free to contractors, transparent multi-lender comparison, and upfront payment

providers.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Acorn Finance

faqs.q: Is Acorn Finance really free for contractors?
faqs.a: Yes — Acorn charges contractors no dealer fee, no subscription, no sign-up fee, and no credit-card fee. You create an account and get a financing link at no cost, and Acorn earns its money from the lender side of the marketplace. That makes it one of the cheapest ways to offer financing.

faqs.q: How does Acorn Finance work for my customers?
faqs.a: The customer uses your financing link, enters basic info, and a soft credit check (no score impact) returns prequalified fixed-rate offers from 30+ lenders in about 60 seconds. They pick the best offer, get funded (within 48 hours of approval), and you're paid upfront for the job.

faqs.q: Acorn Finance vs Hearth?
faqs.a: Both are lender marketplaces with soft-pull prequalification, but Acorn is free to contractors while Hearth charges a $1,499+/year subscription (bundled with sales tools). If you just want free, no-cost financing with multi-lender comparison and upfront payment, Acorn wins on cost; if you want the bundled estimating/sales suite and have the volume, Hearth may justify its fee.

faqs.q: Acorn Finance vs GreenSky?
faqs.a: Acorn is a free marketplace of fixed-rate personal-loan lenders with no dealer fee; GreenSky is an embedded program with 0%/deferred-interest plans up to $100K but dealer fees of 3–15%. Acorn is cheaper for the contractor and offers transparent comparison shopping; GreenSky offers embedded promotional structures and high loan capacity at a higher cost. Many contractors offer a free marketplace like Acorn precisely to avoid dealer fees.

faqs.q: Does the contractor wait for installment payments with Acorn?
faqs.a: No — once the customer accepts an offer and is funded, the contractor is paid upfront, not in installments. The customer repays their chosen lender over time. Lender partners are required to fund within 48 hours of approval, which helps you start jobs faster.

providers.name: Service Finance Company
providers.tagline: Bank-backed (Truist) home-improvement POS financing with 50+ programs
providers.pricing_summary: Single-lender, bank-backed (Truist). 50+ programs spanning promotional (same-as-cash/deferred) and standard installment. Dealer fees and customer APRs are not published — quoted via a sales call.
providers.apr_range: Not published (50+ programs, promotional and standard installment)
providers.loan_range: Not published; broad home-improvement project sizes
providers.contractor_cost: Not published — quoted at enrollment (industry norm is $0 to sign up; promotional/0% programs carry dealer fees)
providers.long_description:
Service Finance Company is one of the safest, broadest contractor-financing choices, precisely because it's bank-backed: owned by Truist (a top-10 US bank) since 2021, it carries far lower counterparty risk than the independent fintechs that have failed in this space. A nationally licensed sales-finance company and FHA Title I lender, it lets home-improvement contractors offer customers point-of-sale installment financing during the in-home sale, with 50+ programs across all 50 states and a network of 8,000+ direct dealers.

The trade-offs are transparency and servicing. Neither dealer fees nor customer APRs are published — you learn them on a sales call — and there's a documented consumer-complaint pattern around promotional-term confusion and interest-vs-principal billing (EnergySage 2.0/5, PissedConsumer 1.8/5), plus weak self-service payment options. It's an A+ BBB letter grade but not accredited. For an established, multi-state contractor who values funding stability and a deep program menu over published pricing, Service Finance is a strong anchor; smaller or single-state shops may prefer a faster-to-enroll independent.
providers.how_it_works: An enrolled dealer presents financing to the homeowner via a portal or mobile app during the sale; the customer is approved, the contractor completes the job, and the contractor is funded. Service Finance underwrites, funds, and services the loan directly. You become a dealer through a contact form and sales call rather than a self-serve signup.

pros_detail.title: Bank-backed stability (Truist)
pros_detail.explanation: Owned by a top-10 US bank, so far lower counterparty risk than the independent financiers that have gone bankrupt in this space.

pros_detail.title: Huge proven dealer network
pros_detail.explanation: 8,000+ direct dealer relationships — a deep, established base, not an unproven startup.

pros_detail.title: Broad 50+ program menu
pros_detail.explanation: Promotional (same-as-cash, deferred) and standard installment options to fit different customers and tickets.

pros_detail.title: Nationwide licensing
pros_detail.explanation: Covers all 50 states under one provider, simplifying multi-state contractor operations.

pros_detail.title: Mobile + portal tooling
pros_detail.explanation: A dedicated dealer app supports on-the-spot in-home applications during the sale.

cons_detail.title: Opaque pricing
cons_detail.explanation: Neither dealer fees nor customer APRs are published — you don't know the cost until a sales call.

cons_detail.title: Consumer complaint pattern
cons_detail.explanation: Documented promotional-term confusion and interest-vs-principal billing complaints (EnergySage 2.0/5, PissedConsumer 1.8/5).

cons_detail.title: Weak self-service payments
cons_detail.explanation: Reviewers report being unable to pay online and having to call — friction that can sour the customer relationship.

cons_detail.title: Not BBB accredited
cons_detail.explanation: Carries an A+ letter grade but is not BBB-accredited, despite the bank ownership.

cons_detail.title: Quoted-vs-approved rate gaps
cons_detail.explanation: Anecdotal reports of differences between quoted and approved rates, which can undercut the in-home pitch.

providers.best_for: Established, multi-state home-improvement contractors (HVAC, windows, doors, siding, roofing, solar) who value bank-backed funding stability and a deep program menu
providers.weaknesses: Opaque dealer-fee/APR pricing (quoted only); consumer complaint pattern (promo confusion, billing); weak self-service payments; not BBB accredited; quoted-vs-approved rate gaps reported
providers.reputation_flag: Documented consumer complaints around promotional-term confusion and interest-vs-principal billing (EnergySage 2.0/5, PissedConsumer 1.8/5), plus weak online payment self-service. Bank-backed and stable (Truist), not defunct — but set clear customer expectations on promo terms.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: valued for bank-backed stability and a broad program menu; consumer-side complaints center on promo-term confusion and billing

providers.affiliate_program: N/A — "become a dealer" via contact form and sales call
providers.affiliate_payout_note: Contractor enrolls as a dealer; not an affiliate-link model

faqs.q: Is Service Finance a safe contractor-financing partner?
faqs.a: Yes on stability — it's owned by Truist, a top-10 US bank, since 2021, which makes it far lower-risk than the independent home-improvement financiers that have gone bankrupt (Mosaic, Sunlight). The caveats are pricing opacity (fees/APRs quoted only) and a consumer-complaint pattern around promotional terms, so set clear customer expectations.

faqs.q: How much does Service Finance cost a contractor?
faqs.a: It doesn't publish dealer fees or customer APRs — you get them on a sales call when you enroll as a dealer. As across the industry, enrolling is typically free and the real cost is the per-deal dealer fee, which rises with richer promotional (0%/same-as-cash) offers. Get the full fee schedule in writing before committing.

faqs.q: What trades does Service Finance serve?
faqs.a: Home-improvement broadly — HVAC, windows, doors, siding, sunrooms, flooring, water treatment, roofing, and solar — across all 50 states. With 8,000+ dealers and 50+ programs it's built for established contractors doing larger-ticket in-home sales rather than small repair tickets.

providers.name: Foundation Finance Company
providers.tagline: Full-credit-spectrum financing — approves customers prime-only lenders decline
providers.pricing_summary: Independent lender, full credit spectrum (FICO as low as 550 via first/second-look). Free, fast dealer enrollment. Low-fee, same-as-cash, deferred, and no-fee promo options; exact dealer fees not published. Loans up to $100,000, terms up to 240 months.
providers.apr_range: Wide — full credit spectrum incl. subprime (high APRs at the low-FICO end)
providers.loan_range: Up to $100,000; terms up to 240 months
providers.contractor_cost: $0 to enroll; dealer fees tiered by promo richness (a testimonial referenced reducing a ~19% fee) with no-fee options available
providers.long_description:
Foundation Finance Company's edge is approvals: it deliberately serves the full credit spectrum, with first- and second-look programs that approve customers down to roughly a 550 FICO — exactly the customers prime-only lenders decline. For a contractor, that translates to higher close rates on jobs that would otherwise die at financing. Enrollment is free and fast (under five minutes), loans go up to $100,000 with terms to 240 months, and there are promo and no-fee dealer options.

The trade-offs follow from the subprime focus: customer APRs at the low-credit end run high (often well above credit cards), dealer fees are undisclosed and can be steep before negotiation (a testimonial referenced ~19%), and there's a transparency/servicing complaint pattern on BBB/Trustpilot around getting payoff information. It's confined to home-improvement verticals and review volume is thin and negatively skewed. For contractors who frequently lose jobs to credit declines — solar, HVAC, roofing, remodeling — Foundation is the standout for capturing credit-challenged customers; for prime-heavy clienteles, a lower-cost lender may fit better.
providers.how_it_works: The customer applies with a soft-pull prequalification (license-scan auto-fill), signs via DocuSign, the contractor completes the job, a verification call confirms it, and the contractor is paid by ACH. First- and second-look programs route declined applicants to additional underwriting to maximize approvals.

pros_detail.title: Approves low credit (down to ~550)
pros_detail.explanation: First/second-look programs capture subprime customers, lifting close rates on jobs that would otherwise fail at financing.

pros_detail.title: Free, fast enrollment
pros_detail.explanation: Sign up free in under five minutes — a low barrier versus the gated big-bank programs.

pros_detail.title: Large ceiling and long terms
pros_detail.explanation: Up to $100,000 and 240-month terms support big-ticket remodels and full-system replacements.

pros_detail.title: Fast ACH funding
pros_detail.explanation: Contractors are paid by ACH after a job-completion verification call.

pros_detail.title: Flexible promos incl. no-fee tiers
pros_detail.explanation: Same-as-cash and deferred options plus no-fee tiers let you protect margin where possible.

cons_detail.title: Subprime focus means high APRs
cons_detail.explanation: Customer rates at the low-FICO end run well above credit-card levels — be transparent with customers.

cons_detail.title: Undisclosed, potentially steep dealer fees
cons_detail.explanation: Dealer fees aren't published and can run high (a testimonial referenced ~19%) before negotiation.

cons_detail.title: Transparency/servicing complaints
cons_detail.explanation: BBB/Trustpilot cite difficulty getting payoff information and other servicing friction.

cons_detail.title: Home-improvement only
cons_detail.explanation: Confined to home-improvement verticals — not general retail or service financing.

cons_detail.title: Thin, skewed review volume
cons_detail.explanation: Limited independent reviews, negatively skewed — harder to gauge typical experience.

providers.best_for: Contractors who frequently lose jobs to credit declines (solar, HVAC, roofing, siding, windows, remodeling, water treatment) and need to approve credit-challenged customers
providers.weaknesses: Subprime focus = high customer APRs; undisclosed, potentially steep dealer fees; transparency/servicing complaints; home-improvement verticals only; thin, negatively-skewed review volume
providers.reputation_flag: Documented complaints around transparency, high APRs at the low-credit end, and promo confusion — typical of subprime lending. Confirmed operating (14+ years, ~300 employees), not defunct — but be upfront with customers about rates and terms.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: valued for approving credit-challenged customers and raising close rates; criticized for high subprime APRs and fee/transparency issues

providers.affiliate_program: N/A — free "Become a Dealer" online application
providers.affiliate_payout_note: Contractor enrolls as a dealer; not an affiliate-link model

faqs.q: What makes Foundation Finance different?
faqs.a: It serves the full credit spectrum — its first- and second-look programs approve customers down to roughly a 550 FICO, the ones prime-only lenders decline. For contractors who regularly lose jobs at the financing step, that higher approval rate is the whole value proposition, even though the trade-off is higher customer APRs at the low end.

faqs.q: How much does Foundation Finance cost a contractor?
faqs.a: Enrolling is free and takes under five minutes. The real cost is the per-deal dealer fee, which Foundation doesn't publish and which scales with promo richness — a customer testimonial referenced negotiating down from a ~19% fee, and no-fee tiers exist. Get your specific fee schedule in writing before relying on it.

faqs.q: What loan sizes does Foundation Finance offer?
faqs.a: Up to $100,000 with terms as long as 240 months, which comfortably covers big-ticket work like full HVAC replacements, roofing, siding, windows, and major remodels. Combined with its low-credit approvals, that makes it useful for closing larger jobs across a wide range of customer credit profiles.

providers.name: Synchrony HOME
providers.tagline: Private-label revolving credit with ~5M cardholders and 0% promos
providers.pricing_summary: Private-label promotional credit card (Synchrony Bank). Dealer fees scale with promo richness (~1%–15%; deep 0% promos 8–15%), often subsidized by manufacturer partnerships (Rheem, York, Mitsubishi, LG). Customer promos 0% APR / deferred-interest; high standard go-to APRs.
providers.apr_range: 0% promotional / deferred-interest; high standard APR after promo (deferred-interest model)
providers.contractor_cost: Dealer fee scales with promo (~1%–15%); manufacturer programs can zero it out. Typically $0 to enroll
providers.long_description:
Synchrony HOME is the brand-name card play: a private-label promotional credit program backed by Synchrony Bank (NYSE: SYF), with roughly 5 million existing cardholders who already recognize and hold the card. Contractors offer it at the point of sale; the customer gets a revolving credit line (cited up to $75,000), the contractor is paid up front, and Synchrony carries the receivable. Its big levers are scale and manufacturer subsidies — OEM programs (Rheem, York, Mitsubishi, LG) can zero out the dealer fee — plus integrations with Payzer and ServiceTitan.

The caveats are the deferred-interest model and underwriting. Deep 0% promos cost the dealer 8–15% absent a subsidy; the card draws a heavy BBB complaint pattern around retroactive deferred interest (customers who miss the promo window get back-charged); underwriting is stricter (~640+ FICO, more declines than full-spectrum lenders); onboarding takes ~1–2 weeks; and it's revolving credit, not an installment loan, with no second-look waterfall. For HVAC, roofing, and remodeling contractors — especially those tied to a participating manufacturer — Synchrony HOME's recognition and 0% promos are compelling; just coach customers carefully on the deferred-interest terms.
providers.how_it_works: The contractor enrolls as a Synchrony merchant, then offers customers a card application in-home or online; approved customers pick a promotional plan (often 0% for 6–60 months). The contractor is paid up front and pays a dealer fee that scales with the promo. Estimates, applications, and payments are managed through Synchrony Transact and the Home Improvement Toolbox.

pros_detail.title: Huge brand and existing cardholder base
pros_detail.explanation: ~5 million Synchrony HOME cardholders already hold the card, so many customers can finance with a card they recognize.

pros_detail.title: Manufacturer-subsidized fees
pros_detail.explanation: OEM programs (Rheem, York, Mitsubishi, LG) can zero out the dealer fee — a real margin advantage if you install those brands.

pros_detail.title: Rich promotional menu
pros_detail.explanation: Many 0%/reduced-APR plans let you tailor the offer per job to close the sale.

pros_detail.title: Software integrations
pros_detail.explanation: Works with Payzer and ServiceTitan plus Synchrony Transact, fitting into existing contractor workflows.

pros_detail.title: High credit lines
pros_detail.explanation: Lines cited up to $75,000 support larger projects on a revolving basis.

cons_detail.title: Dealer fees eat margin
cons_detail.explanation: Deep 0% promos cost 8–15% to the dealer without a manufacturer subsidy — a real hit on bigger tickets.

cons_detail.title: Deferred-interest complaint pattern
cons_detail.explanation: Heavy BBB complaints around retroactive interest when customers miss the promo window — coach customers carefully.

cons_detail.title: Stricter underwriting (~640+ FICO)
cons_detail.explanation: More declines than full-spectrum installment lenders, so credit-challenged customers won't qualify.

cons_detail.title: Slower onboarding
cons_detail.explanation: Becoming a merchant takes ~1–2 weeks versus instant fintech signup.

cons_detail.title: Revolving credit, not an installment loan
cons_detail.explanation: It's a card with no second-look waterfall — a different product from fixed-term installment financing.

providers.best_for: HVAC, roofing, windows/doors, and remodeling contractors — especially those tied to a participating manufacturer whose program subsidizes the dealer fee
providers.weaknesses: Dealer fees 8–15% on deep promos (absent subsidy); deferred-interest consumer complaint pattern; stricter ~640+ FICO underwriting; ~1–2 week onboarding; revolving card, not installment, with no second-look
providers.reputation_flag: Heavy consumer-complaint pattern around deferred-interest retroactive charges (customers back-charged if they miss the 0% window). Publicly traded and stable (SYF), not defunct — but the deferred-interest model requires careful customer coaching.

ratings.bbb: parent not accredited; high deferred-interest complaint volume
ratings.reddit_sentiment: valued for brand recognition and manufacturer-subsidized 0% promos; the recurring issue is deferred-interest back-charges to customers

providers.affiliate_program: N/A — "become a Synchrony merchant/partner" (apply online or by phone)
providers.affiliate_payout_note: Contractor enrolls as a merchant; not an affiliate-link model

faqs.q: Is Synchrony HOME a loan or a credit card?
faqs.a: It's a private-label revolving credit card (issued by Synchrony Bank), not a fixed-term installment loan. The customer gets a credit line they can reuse, with promotional 0%/deferred-interest plans. That's different from installment lenders like Service Finance or Foundation — and it's why the deferred-interest terms matter so much to coach customers on.

faqs.q: How much does Synchrony HOME cost a contractor?
faqs.a: The dealer fee scales with the promo: standard plans are low single digits, while deep 0% promos run roughly 8–15%. The big lever is manufacturer subsidies — if you install a participating brand (Rheem, York, Mitsubishi, LG), their program can zero out your fee. Enrolling as a merchant is typically free.

faqs.q: What is the deferred-interest risk customers complain about?
faqs.a: Many Synchrony HOME promos are deferred-interest: if the customer pays off the balance within the promo window, they pay no interest, but if they don't, interest is charged retroactively from the purchase date. That back-charge is the source of heavy complaints — so set clear expectations with customers about paying off before the window closes.

providers.name: Sunbit
providers.tagline: ~90% approval, 30-second decisions — built for smaller-ticket service jobs
providers.pricing_summary: POS "pay over time" for in-person service businesses. ~5% merchant fee per financed job (some verticals up to 9%), auto-deducted; no monthly/platform fee; contractor paid up front. Lending originated by TAB Bank.
providers.apr_range: 0%–35.99% APR (every approved customer gets a 0% option)
providers.contractor_cost: ~5% per financed transaction (up to 9% some verticals); no monthly or platform fee
providers.long_description:
Sunbit fills the lane the big home-improvement lenders serve poorly: smaller-ticket, high-approval financing for in-person service work. Roughly 90% of applicants are approved in about 30 seconds via a soft credit check, every approved customer gets a 0% option, and the contractor is paid up front with no monthly fee — just a ~5% per-transaction merchant fee. For HVAC service calls, plumbing, electrical, and appliance repair in the $60–$10,000 range, that approval rate and speed close jobs that would stall on a stricter lender.

The trade-offs: the ~5% merchant fee eats more margin than a 0%-dealer-fee home-improvement program on small jobs; the ~$10,000 ceiling rules it out for big remodels, roofs, and full-system replacements; customer APRs reach 35.99% on longer terms after the 0% window; and there's a consumer-complaint cluster around abrupt account 'restrictions' and hard-to-reach support. It's also one of several verticals for Sunbit (it does auto, dental, optical too), not a dedicated home-improvement lender. But for the repair-and-service side of the trades — where Service Finance and Synchrony are overkill — Sunbit is the standout, and it's now embedded in Stripe, Workiz, and Skimmer.
providers.how_it_works: At the point of sale, the customer applies via a soft credit check (no score impact), ~90% are approved in about 30 seconds, optionally make a down payment, then pay fixed monthly installments. Sunbit deducts its ~5% fee and the contractor is paid up front. It's embedded in platforms like Stripe (nationwide GA April 2026), Workiz, and Skimmer.

pros_detail.title: ~90% approval rate
pros_detail.explanation: Far above prime-only lenders, so far more service jobs actually close at the financing step.

pros_detail.title: ~30-second soft-pull decisions
pros_detail.explanation: Near-instant approvals with no credit-score impact keep the customer at the table during the sale.

pros_detail.title: Paid up front, no monthly fee
pros_detail.explanation: Only a per-transaction cost — no platform or monthly fee dragging on slow months.

pros_detail.title: Covers small/mid tickets
pros_detail.explanation: $60–$10,000 financing serves repair and service work many lenders won't touch.

pros_detail.title: Embedded in FSM/payment tools
pros_detail.explanation: Built into Stripe, Workiz, and Skimmer, so offering it can be part of your existing checkout.

cons_detail.title: ~5% merchant fee
cons_detail.explanation: Every financed job costs ~5% (up to 9% some verticals), eating more margin than 0%-dealer-fee home-improvement programs.

cons_detail.title: ~$10,000 ceiling
cons_detail.explanation: Not for $30k–$100k remodels, roofs, or full-system replacements — it's a small/mid-ticket tool.

cons_detail.title: High customer APRs (to 35.99%)
cons_detail.explanation: After the 0% window, longer terms can reach 35.99% APR — be transparent with customers.

cons_detail.title: Account-restriction complaints
cons_detail.explanation: A consumer-complaint cluster around abrupt account 'restrictions' and hard-to-reach support.

cons_detail.title: Not a dedicated home-improvement lender
cons_detail.explanation: Home services is one of several verticals (auto, dental, optical), so it's less specialized than Service Finance/Foundation.

providers.best_for: Smaller-ticket home and field services — HVAC repairs/service calls, plumbing, electrical, pool/spa, appliance repair — that need high approval and instant decisions
providers.weaknesses: ~5% merchant fee eats margin on small jobs; ~$10,000 ceiling rules out big projects; high customer APRs (to 35.99%); account-restriction/support complaints; not a dedicated home-improvement lender
providers.reputation_flag: Consumer-complaint cluster around abrupt account "restrictions" and hard-to-reach support. Confirmed operating and growing (2026 Forbes Fintech 50; Stripe nationwide launch April 2026), not defunct.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: praised by service businesses for ~90% approval and instant decisions on small tickets; the knock is the ~5% fee and occasional account restrictions

providers.affiliate_program: N/A — merchant/partner signup, often via an integrated field-service or payment platform
providers.affiliate_payout_note: Contractor enrolls as a merchant; not an affiliate-link model

faqs.q: What kind of jobs is Sunbit best for?
faqs.a: Smaller-ticket, in-person service work — HVAC service calls and repairs, plumbing, electrical, pool/spa service, and appliance repair, roughly $60–$10,000. Its ~90% approval and 30-second decisions are ideal for closing repair jobs on the spot. It's not for big remodels or full-system replacements, which exceed its ~$10,000 ceiling.

faqs.q: How much does Sunbit cost a contractor?
faqs.a: About 5% per financed transaction (up to 9% in some verticals), auto-deducted from the disbursement, with no monthly or platform fee — and the contractor is paid up front. That per-deal fee is higher than a 0%-dealer-fee home-improvement program, which is the trade-off for the very high approval rate on small tickets.

faqs.q: Does applying for Sunbit hurt my customer’s credit?
faqs.a: No — Sunbit uses a soft credit check to make its decision, so applying doesn't impact the customer's credit score, and about 90% of applicants are approved in roughly 30 seconds. Every approved customer is offered a 0% option, with APRs up to 35.99% on longer terms after the 0% window.

providers.name: Regions | EnerBank Home Improvement Financing
providers.tagline: Direct bank lender (Regions) with no-recourse funding and fast pay
providers.pricing_summary: Direct FDIC-insured bank lender (Regions Bank). Markets "no merchant or hidden add-on fees" and "no sign-up fees" (promotional/0% products carry dealer fees industry-wide; no schedule published — treat as marketing until the program agreement). Products: equal-monthly, low-monthly, Zero Interest, Same-As-Cash.
providers.apr_range: Cited "as low as 8.99%" up to ~24.49% (single-source, partially verified)
providers.contractor_cost: Markets "no sign-up fees"; promotional/0% financing carries dealer fees industry-wide (schedule not published — confirm in the program agreement)
providers.long_description:
Regions | EnerBank (formerly EnerBank USA, now part of Regions Bank) is a direct, FDIC-insured bank lender for home-improvement work — the contractor offers financing at the kitchen table and Regions funds and services the loan in-house, with no recourse to the contractor if the customer later defaults. It's a single-lender model with strong conversion data (Regions markets close-rate lifts from 25% to 44% with same-as-cash plus low-monthly options, and a 69% approval rate), fast contractor payment (~24 hours), and a real tech stack (PreQualify soft pull, mobile app, e-sign).

The main limit is the barrier to entry: Regions requires established contractors — typically 5+ years in business and $500k+ in sales (or 3 years/$300k with a Regions relationship) — which excludes new or small shops. There's also a ~$5,000 minimum average project, undisclosed promo dealer-fee transparency, a single-lender (no second-look) model, and a high APR ceiling (~24.49%). For established remodeling trades — HVAC, windows, roofing, decks, kitchen & bath — that want bank stability, no-recourse funding, and fast pay, it's excellent; newer or small-ticket contractors should look elsewhere.
providers.how_it_works: The contractor enrolls as an approved partner, then presents loan options via a PreQualify soft-pull tool, mobile app, online, or phone. Regions underwrites, funds, and services every loan directly, paying the contractor typically within ~24 hours of project milestones, with no recourse if the customer defaults. Onboarding can take as little as three weeks.

pros_detail.title: No recourse on default
pros_detail.explanation: Funds aren't clawed back if the customer later defaults — the lending risk sits with Regions, not the contractor.

pros_detail.title: Bank stability, direct lender
pros_detail.explanation: Regions (an FDIC-insured bank) funds and services in-house — no fintech counterparty risk.

pros_detail.title: Fast contractor payment
pros_detail.explanation: Contractors are typically paid within ~24 hours of project milestones.

pros_detail.title: Strong conversion data
pros_detail.explanation: Regions cites close-rate lifts from 25% to 44% with same-as-cash + low-monthly options, and a 69% approval rate.

pros_detail.title: Real tech stack
pros_detail.explanation: PreQualify soft pull, mobile app, and e-sign, with onboarding in as little as three weeks.

cons_detail.title: High barrier to entry
cons_detail.explanation: Typically requires 5+ years in business / $500k+ sales (or 3 yrs/$300k with a Regions relationship) — excludes new/small contractors.

cons_detail.title: ~$5,000 minimum project
cons_detail.explanation: Oriented to larger jobs; not for small repair/service tickets.

cons_detail.title: Dealer-fee transparency
cons_detail.explanation: Promotional financing dealer fees aren't published despite the 'no fees' marketing — confirm in the agreement.

cons_detail.title: Single-lender, no second-look
cons_detail.explanation: Declined customers aren't shopped to a second lender — lower capture than full-spectrum platforms.

cons_detail.title: High APR ceiling (~24.49%)
cons_detail.explanation: Residential-remodel financing with rates that can reach ~24.49% at the top end.

providers.best_for: Established remodeling trades (HVAC, windows, roofing, decks, kitchen & bath) doing ~$5,000+ projects that want bank stability, no-recourse funding, and fast pay
providers.weaknesses: High barrier to entry (5+ yrs / $500k+ sales); ~$5,000 minimum project; promo dealer-fee transparency; single-lender (no second-look); high APR ceiling (~24.49%)

ratings.reddit_sentiment: regarded as a stable, established bank lender with no-recourse funding and fast pay; main gripe is the high barrier to enroll

providers.affiliate_payout_note: Contractor enrolls as an approved partner; not an affiliate-link model

faqs.q: What does "no recourse" mean with Regions/EnerBank?
faqs.a: It means if the homeowner later defaults on the loan, Regions doesn't claw back the money it already paid you — the credit risk sits with the bank, not the contractor. Combined with payment within ~24 hours of milestones, that makes the contractor's cash position clean and predictable, which is a key reason established remodelers use it.

faqs.q: Can any contractor sign up for Regions/EnerBank?
faqs.a: No — it's gated to established contractors, typically requiring 5+ years in business and $500k+ in annual sales (or 3 years and $300k with an existing Regions relationship). New or small shops generally won't qualify and should look at lower-barrier options like Foundation Finance or Sunbit. There's also a ~$5,000 minimum average project size.

faqs.q: What does Regions/EnerBank cost a contractor?
faqs.a: It markets 'no sign-up fees' and 'no merchant or hidden add-on fees,' but as across the industry its promotional and 0% products carry dealer fees that aren't published — so treat the 'no fees' language as marketing for the standard products and confirm the full fee schedule in your program agreement before relying on it.

providers.name: PowerPay
providers.tagline: Multi-lender POS platform for large-ticket jobs — staged funding to $100k
providers.pricing_summary: Multi-lender POS platform (funds via partner lenders). Markets low/no contractor fees vs the "average 7.5%" industry norm (exact schedule not published). Loans up to $100,000, terms up to 15 years; staged/progress funding + rate-buydown options.
providers.loan_range: Up to $100,000; terms up to 15 years (derived avg ~$17.5K)
providers.contractor_cost: Markets low/no contractor fees (vs ~7.5% industry norm); exact dealer-fee schedule not published
providers.long_description:
PowerPay is a digital, multi-lender point-of-sale platform built for larger-ticket home-improvement (and elective-medical) financing: a contractor's customer applies once and PowerPay routes them to a network of funding partners, with unsecured loans up to $100,000, terms up to 15 years, staged/progress funding for long projects, and rate-buydown options. It markets low-or-no contractor fees against the industry's ~7.5%-dealer-fee norm and has funded $2.1B+ across ~120,000 transactions in all 50 states. For big-ticket roofing, full HVAC, windows, and remodels, the high loan ceiling and progress funding are the draw.

The caveats: public pricing is opaque (no fee schedule or full APR matrix), it's unsecured and credit-driven so the best rates/amounts skew to stronger credit (it lacks Sunbit's ~90% soft-pull breadth), and there's a consumer-complaint cluster around interest charged before promos end, missing statements, and slow refunds (with conflicting BBB-grade reports). It also splits focus with elective-medical financing rather than being a single-vertical specialist. For mid-to-large home-improvement jobs where loan size and progress funding matter, PowerPay is a strong platform — verify your specific fees and the customer experience before leaning on it.
providers.how_it_works: A contractor (one of 12,000+ underwritten providers) presents PowerPay at the point of sale; the customer applies digitally for an unsecured installment loan up to $100,000, which funds through partner lenders with terms up to 15 years. The platform supports staged/progress funding (disbursing as the job advances) and rate buydowns, fully paperless with fast approvals.

pros_detail.title: Large loan capacity (to $100,000)
pros_detail.explanation: Supports big-ticket roofing, full HVAC, windows, and major remodels that exceed small-ticket lenders' limits.

pros_detail.title: Low contractor-fee positioning
pros_detail.explanation: Markets away from the ~7.5% dealer-fee norm, aiming to protect contractor margin (verify your actual fees).

pros_detail.title: Staged/progress funding
pros_detail.explanation: Disburses as the job progresses, improving cash flow on long, large projects.

pros_detail.title: Competitive customer APRs + buydowns
pros_detail.explanation: Rates start around 6.99–8.99% with rate-buydown options to sharpen the customer offer.

pros_detail.title: Broad coverage, fully paperless
pros_detail.explanation: Nationwide across many trades with fast, paperless approvals through a multi-lender network.

cons_detail.title: Opaque public pricing
cons_detail.explanation: No published fee schedule or full APR matrix — you confirm your costs only on enrollment.

cons_detail.title: Unsecured, credit-driven
cons_detail.explanation: Best rates and amounts skew to stronger credit; it lacks Sunbit's ~90% soft-pull approval breadth.

cons_detail.title: Consumer complaint cluster
cons_detail.explanation: Reports of interest charged before promos end, missing statements, and slow refunds, with conflicting BBB-grade reports.

cons_detail.title: Split focus with medical financing
cons_detail.explanation: Also does elective-medical/patient financing, so it's not a single-vertical home-improvement specialist.

cons_detail.title: Not for small tickets
cons_detail.explanation: Oriented to mid/large loans — overkill for repair-and-service work Sunbit handles better.

providers.best_for: Mid-to-large home-improvement trades (roofing, siding, windows/doors, HVAC replacement, decking, remodeling) needing high loan amounts and progress funding
providers.weaknesses: Opaque public pricing; unsecured/credit-driven (lower approval breadth than Sunbit); consumer complaints (pre-promo interest, statements, refunds); split focus with medical financing; not for small tickets
providers.reputation_flag: Consumer-complaint cluster around interest charged before promos end, missing statements, and slow refunds, with conflicting BBB-grade reports. Confirmed operating ($556M securitization Oct 2025; $2.1B+ funded), not defunct — verify your fees and the customer experience.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: valued for large loan amounts and progress funding on big jobs; criticized for opaque pricing and pre-promo interest/refund complaints

providers.affiliate_program: N/A — merchant/service-provider underwriting (contractor applies to be approved)
providers.affiliate_payout_note: Contractor enrolls as an approved provider; not an affiliate-link model. Correct domain is getpowerpay.com

faqs.q: How is PowerPay different from a single lender?
faqs.a: PowerPay is a multi-lender marketplace platform — the customer applies once and PowerPay routes them across a network of funding partners, rather than approving against one lender's box. Combined with loans up to $100,000 and terms to 15 years plus staged funding, that makes it well-suited to large home-improvement projects, though the best rates still skew to stronger-credit customers.

faqs.q: What does PowerPay cost a contractor?
faqs.a: PowerPay markets low or no contractor fees compared to the industry's roughly 7.5% dealer-fee norm, but it doesn't publish an exact fee schedule or full APR matrix — so confirm your specific costs during enrollment. Customer APRs start around 6.99–8.99%, with rate-buydown options available to sharpen offers.

faqs.q: What size jobs is PowerPay best for?
faqs.a: Mid-to-large projects — roofing, siding, windows and doors, full HVAC replacements, decking, and major remodels — thanks to its $100,000 ceiling, 15-year terms, and staged/progress funding that disburses as the work advances. For small repair tickets, a high-approval small-ticket option like Sunbit is a better fit.
